source.title: Inflexible neurobiological signatures precede atypical development in infants at high risk for autism

confidence_rationale: Nature Scientific Reports study; n=71 infants (high-risk HR and low-risk LR for ASD) with Motion Capture from 1-2 months; validated against a 1,445 infant sibling dataset. Key finding is that context-inflexibility (not raw variability level) is the pathological signature. This directly bears on REE's diversity mechanism design: the target is context-appropriate variability, not maximum entropy. High source quality; mapping fidelity strong because the operationalization of healthy vs pathological exploration maps directly to REE's diversity acceptance criteria.
failure_signatures: The HR group does not uniformly develop ASD -- only a subset, limiting causal inference.
Head movement is used as the primary motor proxy, which may not generalize to limb or whole-body motor variability patterns.
The ASD pathway may be atypical in ways specific to social development; the logic may not transfer cleanly to non-social behavioral flexibility.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Context-inflexible sensorimotor systems at 1-2 months predict atypical developmental trajectory. Healthy LR infants' movements differ significantly between language listening and sleep; HR infants' movements are similar in both conditions -- a striking lack of contextual diversity rather than a flat variability deficit.
mapping.ree_translation: The critical infant-stage metric is not action entropy per se but context-modulated variability -- the same finding REE's behavioral diversity acceptance criteria Rung 2 requires (context-sensitive switching). An infant REE substrate that produces high entropy uniformly across contexts would be pathological by this standard. REE needs an environmental context signal that modulates exploration amplitude.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The ASD risk framing introduces clinical confounds (genetic predisposition, atypical social brain development). The core finding -- context-inflexibility as a failure mode -- is robustly transferable, but the etiology may not be.
mapping.source_context: Human infant longitudinal; Columbia University cohort; motion capture of spontaneous head movements during language listening and sleep; validated against Cambridge baby-sibling cohort.

confidence_components.notes: Strong empirical result in Scientific Reports with replication cohort; transfer risk is moderate because ASD-specific mechanisms may not generalize.
